records.ety: [AS. dim; akin to OFries. dim, Icel. dimmr: cf. MHG. timmer, timber; of uncertain origin.]

defs: Not bright or distinct; wanting luminousness or clearness; obscure in luster or sound; dusky; darkish; obscure; indistinct; overcast; tarnished.
quotes: How is the gold become dim!
I never saw The heavens so dim by day.
Three sleepless nights I passed in sounding on, Through words and things, a dim and perilous way.

defs: Of obscure vision; not seeing clearly; hence, dull of apprehension; of weak perception; obtuse.
quotes: Mine eye also is dim by reason of sorrow.

records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Dimmed (?); p. pr. & vb. n. Dimming.]

defs: To render dim, obscure, or dark; to make less bright or distinct; to take away the luster of; to darken; to dull; to obscure; to eclipse.
quotes: A king among his courtiers, who dims all his attendants.
Now set the sun, and twilight dimmed the ways.

defs: To deprive of distinct vision; to hinder from seeing clearly, either by dazzling or clouding the eyes; to darken the senses or understanding of.
quotes: Her starry eyes were dimmed with streaming tears.
